summary: Sawtelle used to be a vibrant Japanese American hub, especially in the 1920s and 30s, but over time it has mostly disappeared as a JA center. The LA City Council recognized Sawtelle Japantown just two years ago, but that feels like the tail-end of its prominence. After World War II, Sawtelle became a boomtown in the late 40s and 50s, serving as a place where many Japanese Americans rebuilt their lives and businesses. My family had connections there too, like the Sawtelle Fish Market where I met my wife.

The Los Angeles City Council officially recognized Sawtelle Japantown just two years ago, which was a significant but somewhat late acknowledgment of the area's Japanese American heritage. This recognition came long after Sawtelle had lost much of its identity as a Japanese American hub. It was important because it gave some formal respect to the community's history in Los Angeles.

Westwood was one of the neighborhoods around Sawtelle, part of the greater Los Angeles area where Japanese Americans lived and worked. It was nearby Sawtelle and other affluent areas like Bel Air and Brentwood. These areas were part of the landscape where Japanese Americans were often relegated to service roles like gardening and landscaping due to redlining and segregation.

Bel Air was one of the affluent neighborhoods near Sawtelle, Westwood, and Brentwood where Japanese Americans were often hired to take care of landscaping and gardening. Despite working in these wealthy areas, the Japanese American community was segregated and lived separately, often in places like Sawtelle. This dynamic was part of the racial and social structure in Los Angeles before and after the war.

Brentwood was another neighborhood adjacent to Sawtelle and Bel Air where Japanese Americans found work, particularly in gardening and landscaping. Like Westwood and Bel Air, Brentwood represented the areas where Japanese Americans were allowed to work but not necessarily live. This was part of the broader pattern of redlining and social segregation in Los Angeles.

Pacific Palisades was one of the upscale neighborhoods near Sawtelle where Japanese Americans were employed to maintain landscapes and gardens. It was part of the cluster of affluent areas surrounding Sawtelle, which was the designated area for Japanese American residents and workers. This geographic and social separation was a clear example of the racial boundaries in Los Angeles at the time.

The Nisei were second-generation Japanese Americans who played a crucial role in rebuilding the community after World War II. They were often more entrepreneurial and were the driving force behind organizations like the Japanese American Citizens League, which was dominated by Nisei leadership. The Nisei also had tensions with the Issei and Kibei generations, especially during and after the war, as they sometimes excluded Issei from leadership roles.

The Kibei were Japanese Americans who were born in the U.S. but sent to Japan for education before returning. My father wanted me to become a Kibei and get educated in Japan before coming back to America. However, with the war approaching, it was fortunate that I didn't go because I might have been conscripted into the Japanese army. The Kibei often had a complex identity, caught between Japan and the U.S., and sometimes clashed with the Nisei.

The Issei were the first-generation Japanese immigrants who often faced language barriers and discrimination. After the war, many Issei couldn't speak English well, so the Nisei had to step up and take care of business and negotiations. The Issei were often excluded from leadership in organizations like the JACL, which caused resentment. My father was Issei and had a tough life, including running a laundry and making geta shoes.

My family originally came from Hiroshima Prefecture in Japan. My grandfather even went back there and acted like a big kingpin, though he struggled with alcoholism and illness. Hiroshima was part of our heritage, and after the war, I was offered a job with the Atomic Bomb Casualty Commission in Hiroshima, which would have been a very different life if I had taken it. So Hiroshima has always been a significant place in my family's story.
cues: Atomic Bomb Casualty Commission job offer

summary: The Japanese American Citizens League, or JACL, was dominated by Nisei leadership after the war and purposely excluded the Issei from participation. This caused a lot of resentment within the community, especially among the Issei and Kibei. The JACL was seen as the establishment, and during incidents like the Manzanar and Poston riots, there was direct conflict between JACL members and other segments of the community. The JACL played a significant role in shaping post-war Japanese American identity.

Manzanar was one of the internment camps where Japanese Americans were held during World War II. It was also the site of the Manzanar riot, which was prompted by tensions between the Nisei-led JACL and other groups like the Kibei. My family experienced the camp life, and the camp environment forced many changes in identity and community dynamics. Manzanar remains a powerful symbol of that difficult period.

The Manzanar riot was a significant event during the internment period where Kibei individuals clashed with the Nisei-led JACL members. It reflected the deep divisions within the Japanese American community over loyalty and leadership during the war. This riot was one of several, including the Poston riot, that showed how fraught the camp experience was beyond just the physical confinement.

Poston was another internment camp where my family was held during World War II. Like Manzanar, it was a place of hardship and tension, including a riot similar to the one at Manzanar. The camp was very hot, and my mother nearly died from the heat during the first year. Life at Poston shaped much of our family's experience and identity during those years.

The Shin-Issei were the new wave of Japanese immigrants who came to the U.S. around the mid-1960s, just before the economic boom in Japan during the 1970s. Their arrival marked a new chapter for the Japanese American community, bringing fresh energy and connections to Japan. This contrasted with the earlier generations who had been more focused on assimilation and rebuilding after the war.

My family lived in Northern San Diego, where my dad and uncle were strawberry farmers before the war. We were sent to camp with three cousins during the internment. Growing up in San Diego shaped much of my early life before I moved into academics and later military service. It was a different environment from Los Angeles but still part of the broader Japanese American experience.

Tak Fujimoto is my younger brother, who became a famous cinematographer. We weren't very close growing up because we were like two different families, with me and my older siblings born in the 20s and 30s, and Tak and the younger ones born later. Tak grew up more Americanized and didn't know much about Japan or the language, unlike me, who was encouraged to become a Kibei. Our family dynamic was quite complex, but Tak made his own path in the arts.

I graduated from UCLA right after the Korean War started, around 1950. I volunteered for the army and got caught up in the Korean War, which changed my career plans. Instead of going into engineering, I shifted toward management after my military service. The war was a pivotal moment that influenced my life direction.

Poston was the internment camp where my family was held during World War II. It was extremely hot, and my mother nearly died from the heat during the first year. Life there was difficult but I was young and managed to have some fun despite the circumstances. Poston also experienced a riot, reflecting the tensions within the camp community.

The 442nd Infantry Regiment was highly glorified in the Japanese American community for their service during World War II. Many people celebrated those who served in the 442nd and 100th Infantry Battalion as heroes. However, there were also those who resisted military service, like Frank Emi and the no-no resistance group, which caused divisions. The 442nd symbolized loyalty and sacrifice for many Nisei.

The 100th Infantry Battalion, along with the 442nd Infantry Regiment, was part of the celebrated Japanese American military units during World War II. Their service was highly regarded and became a source of pride for the community. These units represented the loyalty of Japanese Americans despite the hardships of internment. Their legacy is still remembered today.

Frank Emi was a leader of the no-no resistance group during the internment, opposing the loyalty questionnaire and military service. He and others like him were at odds with the JACL and those who served in the military. This resistance led to Tule Lake becoming a segregation camp for those labeled disloyal. Frank Emi's role highlights the complex and sometimes contentious nature of loyalty and identity during the war.

Tule Lake was the segregation camp during World War II where those considered disloyal or part of the no-no resistance group were sent. It was a place of division within the Japanese American community, separating those who resisted from those who complied. The camp symbolizes the internal conflicts and struggles over loyalty and identity during the internment period.

I attended UCLA for my higher education, earning all my degrees there mostly in management. I initially planned to study engineering and was even accepted to UC Berkeley's engineering school but chose UCLA instead. My time at UCLA was formative, and after graduating, I received a job offer from the Atomic Bomb Casualty Commission. UCLA was a key part of my academic and professional development.

I was accepted to the engineering school at UC Berkeley but ultimately decided not to attend. Instead, I went to UCLA and later joined the army during the Korean War. Berkeley was part of my early academic options, but my path changed due to the war and my evolving interests. It was a significant choice point in my life.

After graduating from UCLA, I was offered a position as personnel director at the Atomic Bomb Casualty Commission in Hiroshima. It was a prestigious opportunity, but I couldn't take it because my future wife's father wouldn't allow me to take her to Japan before her sister was married. This job offer represented a crossroads between my Japanese heritage and American life. It would have led to a very different life path.

Hiroshima is where my family originated, and it was also the city devastated by the atomic bomb during World War II. I was offered a job there with the Atomic Bomb Casualty Commission, which would have taken me back to my ancestral home. However, family traditions prevented me from taking the job and moving there with my wife. Hiroshima remains a significant place in my family's history and identity.

Bob Fujimoto was the owner of the gas station where I worked when I first met my wife. We didn't know each other before that, but I was pumping gas for her one day and that's how we connected. Bob was not related to us, but his gas station on Sawtelle was the place where our paths crossed. It was a simple moment that led to a lifelong relationship.
